elements.sent-2411-001: In this paper, we evaluate the potential of multiband gravitational wave observations to constrain the properties of static dark matter spikes around intermediate-mass ratio inspirals.
elements.sent-2411-002: The influence of dark matter on the orbital evolution of the compact binary is incorporated as a correction to the inspiral Newtonian gravitational waveform.
elements.sent-2411-003: We show that the observations from the proposed space-based detector GWSat, sensitive within the deci-Hz frequency band, when combined with that of the third-generation ground-based detectors like the Einstein Telescope and Cosmic Explorer, will produce significantly improved error estimates for all parameters.
elements.sent-2411-004: In particular, our results demonstrate that the joint multiband approach substantially refines the bounds on the dark matter spike parameters-namely, the power-law index and spike density-by factors of approximately $10^6$ and $10^3$, respectively, compared to observations employing only third-generation gravitational wave detectors.
elements.sent-2411-005: The direct observation of gravitational waves (GWs) has significantly enhanced our understanding of the universe, particularly in the study of black holes (BHs) and neutron stars (NSs).
elements.sent-2411-006: The detection of the first binary BH merger, GW150914, by the Laser Interferometer Gravitational-Wave Observatory (LIGO) in 2015 marked the beginning of GW astronomy~\cite{PhysRevLett.116.061102 , which has since led to a growing catalog of detected binary mergers.}
elements.sent-2411-007: Looking ahead, the next generation of GW detectors promises to further expand our capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-008: Ground-based GW observatories such as the Cosmic Explorer (CE)~\cite{Reitze:2019iox and Einstein Telescope(ET)~\cite{Punturo:2010zz , with their increased sensitivity, will enable the detection of fainter and more distant sources.}}
elements.sent-2411-009: Additionally, planned space-based detectors like LISA~\cite{LISA:2017pwj , DECIGO~\cite{Yagi:2011wg , and GWSat—a proposed Indian space-based GW detector—will aim to capture lower-frequency GW signals, particularly those emitted during the early inspiral phase of massive compact binary systems.}}
elements.sent-2411-010: These advancements in detector technology will provide a more complete picture of the universe's compact objects and allow us to probe new physics, such as the nature of dark matter (DM), by exploring the environments in which these binaries exist.
elements.sent-2411-011: So far, most analyses of GW signals have assumed that source binaries evolve in vacuum environments, free from external perturbations.
elements.sent-2411-012: This assumption has served well for the initial detection of binary mergers by the LIGO-Virgo-KAGRA (LVK) collaboration~\cite{LIGOScientific:2018mvr, LIGOScientific:2020ibl, KAGRA:2021vkt, LIGOScientific:2018jsj, KAGRA:2021duu , but it overlooks the possibility that many compact binaries could reside in dense astrophysical environments that might influence their orbital dynamics~\cite{Barausse:2014tra, Barausse:2014pra .}}
elements.sent-2411-013: Recent theoretical developments suggest that compact binaries, particularly those involving massive black holes, may be embedded in DM halos or other dense astrophysical structures~\cite{Merritt:2002vj .}
elements.sent-2411-014: Such environments could measurably impact the emitted GWs, altering the observed waveforms and providing indirect evidence of the presence and distribution of dark matter~\cite{Macedo:2013qea, Eda:2013gg, Eda:2014kra, Yue:2017iwc,Yue:2018vtk,Hannuksela:2019vip,Cardoso:2019rou,Kavanagh:2020cfn,Coogan:2021uqv,PhysRevD.104.104042,PhysRevD.104.124082,Becker:2022wlo,Baryakhtar:2022hbu,Cardoso:2022whc,Singh:2022wvw,Destounis:2022obl,Nichols:2023ufs, AbhishekChowdhuri:2023rfv, Bhattacharyya:2023kbh, Rahman:2023sof,Duque:2023seg,CanevaSantoro:2023aol,Speeney:2024mas,Aurrekoetxea:2024cqd,Zhang:2024ugv,Bertone:2024wbn,Spieksma:2024voy,Duque:2024mfw,Cheng:2024mgl .}
elements.sent-2411-015: Navarro, Frenk, and White (NFW) were the first to demonstrate through cosmological N-body numerical simulations that DM halos possess a universal equilibrium density profile, commonly known as the NFW profile~\cite{Navarro:1996gj .}
elements.sent-2411-016: Subsequently, Gondolo and Silk indicated that the adiabatic growth of supermassive black holes (SMBHs) could significantly alter the surrounding DM distribution, leading to the formation of DM overdensities known as DM spikes~\cite{Gondolo:1999ef .}
elements.sent-2411-017: However, host galaxy mergers and other astrophysical processes could disrupt these spike structures around SMBHs~\cite{Ullio:2001fb, Vasiliev:2008uz, Bertone:2005xv .}
elements.sent-2411-018: In contrast, intermediate-mass black holes (IMBHs), which have masses ranging from $10^2$ to $10^5 \rm M_{\odot}$, are less likely to experience such disruptions, making them ideal candidates for investigating DM spikes~\cite{Zhao:2005zr, Bertone:2005xz .}
elements.sent-2411-019: The existence of DM spikes can influence the orbital evolution of compact binaries.
elements.sent-2411-020: When a stellar-mass compact object inspirals around an IMBH embedded in a DM spike, it experiences not only the gravitational pull of the BH but also a drag force known as dynamical friction, which results from interactions with the dark matter spike~\cite{Chandrasekhar:1943ys, Ostriker:1998fa, Kim:2007zb .}
elements.sent-2411-021: This dynamical friction alters the binary's inspiral rate, resulting in phase shifts in the gravitational waveform relative to what would be observed in a vacuum.
elements.sent-2411-022: Intermediate-mass ratio inspirals (IMRIs), which involve a stellar-mass object spiraling into an IMBH, are characterized by mass ratios in the range of $q = m_2/m_1 \sim 10^{-3 -10^{-5}$, with $m_1$ and $m_2$ representing the masses of the IMBH and the smaller companion, respectively.}
elements.sent-2411-023: Space-based detectors such as LISA, DECIGO, and GWSat are going to be particularly valuable for detecting the early inspiral phase of IMRIs, during which the influence of the DM spike is most significant.
elements.sent-2411-024: These detectors operate in a lower frequency range (millihertz to decihertz) compared to ground-based detectors, making them ideal for capturing the initial stages of the inspiral when the orbital velocity is lower and the DM environment has a significant impact on the binary's evolution.
elements.sent-2411-025: By combining data from both space-based and ground-based detectors—a technique known as multiband gravitational wave astronomy—it will be possible to track these systems across a broad range of frequencies, from the early inspiral phase to the final merger.
elements.sent-2411-026: This approach will significantly enhance parameter estimation and allow for more precise measurements of dark matter properties.
elements.sent-2411-027: Multiband observations have been shown to dramatically enhance the precision of parameter estimation for compact binaries \cite{Sesana:2016ljz,Datta:2020vcj .}
elements.sent-2411-028: Previous studies have primarily focused on stellar-mass binary black holes like GW150914, showing that multiband observations can improve signal-to-noise ratios by several orders of magnitude compared to single-band observations~\cite{Sesana:2016ljz,Barausse:2016eii, Gnocchi:2019jzp, Grimm:2020ivq .}
elements.sent-2411-029: In the case of IMRIs, the advantages of multiband detection are even more pronounced.
elements.sent-2411-030: IMRIs may produce detectable signals in both space-based and ground-based detectors, with signal-to-noise ratios of the order of hundreds to thousands in the space-based band (e.g., GWSat) and tens in the ground-based band (e.g., ET/CE).
elements.sent-2411-031: This complementary capability allows for reduced uncertainties in the measurement of key parameters~\cite{Datta:2020vcj , including the characteristics of DM spikes.}
elements.sent-2411-032: A numerical relativity study of GW150914-like events similarly suggests that environmental effects can mimic vacuum waveforms, potentially biasing parameter estimates \cite{Roy:2024rhe .}
elements.sent-2411-033: Moreover, Ref.~\cite{Wilcox:2024sqs demonstrated that using vacuum waveform templates for GW from inspirals in dark matter halos leads to biased parameter estimation, and suggests the necessity for an expanded parametrized post-Einsteinian (ppE) framework or more specialized DM waveform models.}
elements.sent-2411-034: In this work, we investigate the potential of multiband GW observations to measure the properties of static DM spikes around IMRIs using a Fisher analysis framework.
elements.sent-2411-035: We assess how the complementary sensitivities of space-based and ground-based detectors can significantly improve parameter estimation for both the vacuum and DM spike model parameters.
elements.sent-2411-036: Our results demonstrate that multiband observations provide significantly tighter constraints on the spike density and the power-law index compared to single-band observations, offering a promising avenue for probing dark matter environments around IMBHs.
elements.sent-2411-037: This paper is structured as follows: In Sec.~\ref{sec:DM_spike_model , we describe the waveform models used for IMRIs in a DM environment and for parameter estimation.}
elements.sent-2411-038: In Sec~\ref{sec:Multiband_fisher , we outline the multiband Fisher analysis methodology.}
elements.sent-2411-039: Sec.~\ref{sec:results presents the results obtained, which is followed by concluding remarks in Sec.~\ref{sec:conclusion .}}
elements.sent-2411-040: Throughout the paper, we adopt the geometrized units, where G = c = 1.
elements.section-2411-002: Gravitational waves from the evolution of the IMRI in static dark matter spike
elements.sent-2411-041: We consider an IMRI system comprising a stellar-mass compact object spiraling into an IMBH that is enveloped by a static DM spike, where the density profile remains constant over time.
elements.sent-2411-042: The mass of the IMBH is denoted by $m_1$, while the mass of the small compact object is indicated by $m_2$, and the orbital radius of the latter is symbolized by $r_2$.
elements.sent-2411-043: Initially, if the DM halo initially exhibits a cuspy profile $\rho(r)\propto r^{-\alpha}$ with $0 \leq \alpha \leq 2$, this DM distribution will be altered due to adiabatic growth of the central IMBH, leading to the formation of a dense spike.
elements.sent-2411-044: Following~\cite{Eda:2014kra , we consider the spherically symmetric DM spike distribution with a simple power law: \begin{equation}\rho_{\text{DM}}(r) = \begin{cases} \rho_{\text{sp}} \left( \frac{r_{\text{sp}}}{r} \right)^{\gamma_{\text{sp}}} \text{for } r_{\text{in}} \leq r \leq r_{\text{sp}} 0 \text{for } r < r_{\text{in}} \end{cases}, \label{placeholder}\end{equation} where $r$ denotes the distance from the center of the IMBH.}
elements.formula-2411-001: \begin{equation}\rho_{DM}(r) =  \rho_{sp} \left( \frac{r_{sp}}{r} \right)^{\gamma_{sp}} for  r_{in} \leq r \leq r_{sp} 0 for  r < r_{in} , \label{placeholder}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-045: Following~\cite{Sadeghian:2013laa , we define the inner radius of the spike as $r_{\text{in = 4 G m_1/c^2$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-046: The maximum radius of the spike $r_{\mathrm{sp }$ is determined by $r_{\mathrm{sp }\sim 0.2 r_h$, where $r_h$ is the radius of the gravitational influence of the central BH defined by \begin{equation}\int_{r_{\text{in}}}^{r_{h}} \rho_{\text{DM}}(r) \, d^3r = 2m_1. \label{eq:r_h}\end{equation} From this, the maximum radius of the spike $r_{\text{sp }$ is determined as \begin{equation}r_{\text{sp}} \approx \left[ \frac{(3 - \gamma_{\text{sp}}) 0.2^{3 - \gamma_{\text{sp}}} m_1}{2 \pi \rho_{\text{sp}}} \right]^{1/3}. \label{eq:r_sp}\end{equation} The power-law index $\gamma_{\mathrm{sp }$ that describes the final halo profile depends on the initial slope of the DM halo $\alpha$ which is related to the history of the formation of the IMBH via relation \begin{equation}\gamma_{\rm sp} = \frac{9 - 2\alpha}{4 - \alpha}\,. \label{eq:gamma_sp}\end{equation} For the NFW profile, the initial slope is $\alpha=1$, henceforth the slope $\gamma_{\mathrm{sp =7/3=2.3\bar3$ is anticipated to form at the halo's center~\cite{Navarro:1996gj .}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-047: Following the setting used in~\cite{Eda:2014kra, Eda:2013gg , we set $\rho_{\mathrm{sp = 226 \mathrm{M _{\odot /\mathrm{pc ^3$ and $\gamma_{\mathrm{sp =7/3$ throughout this paper.}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-048: As a smaller compact object traverses a DM spike, the surrounding DM particles generate a disturbance that applies a drag force on it.
elements.sent-2411-049: This force reduces the smaller object's orbital speed, resulting in energy loss via dynamical friction (DF)~\cite{Chandrasekhar:1943ys .}
elements.sent-2411-050: This energy dissipation hastens the orbital decay of the smaller body, causing it to spiral inward at a rate faster than in the absence of matter.
elements.sent-2411-051: Apart from DF, the system also loses energy through gravitational wave emission.
elements.sent-2411-052: The overall rate at which the system's orbital energy decreases can be described as \cite{Coogan:2021uqv , \begin{equation}\frac{\mathrm{d}E_{\text{orb}}}{\mathrm{d}t} = -\frac{\mathrm{d}E_{\text{GW}}}{\mathrm{d}t} - \frac{\mathrm{d}E_{\text{DF}}}{\mathrm{d}t}. \label{eq:energy_balance}\end{equation} The rate of energy loss due to GW emission for a circular orbit in the quadrupole approximation is expressed as \cite{Maggiore:2007ulw : \begin{equation}\frac{\mathrm{d}E_{\mathrm{GW}}}{\mathrm{d}t} = \frac{32G^4 M (m_1 m_2)^2}{5(cr_2)^5}. \label{eq:dE_GW}\end{equation} The energy loss because of dynamical friction is given by \cite{Coogan:2021uqv, Chandrasekhar:1943ys , \begin{equation}\frac{\mathrm{d}E_{DF}}{\mathrm{d}t} = 4\pi (Gm_2)^2 \rho_{\mathrm{DM}}(r_2) \xi(v) v^{-1} \log \Lambda, \label{eq:dE_DF}\end{equation} where $v$ denotes the orbital velocity and the term $\xi(v)$ represents the fraction of dark matter particles with velocities lower than the orbital speed of the secondary object.}}}
elements.sent-2411-053: As shown in~\cite{Nichols:2023ufs , the factor $\xi$ is expressed as \begin{equation}\xi = 1 - I_{1/2} \left( \gamma_{sp} - \frac{1}{2}, \frac{3}{2} \right), \label{eq:xi}\end{equation} where $I_{1/2}$ is the regularized incomplete beta function which estimated to be $\xi \approx 0.58$ for $\gamma_{\mathrm{sp = 7/3$ as discussed in~\cite{Kavanagh:2020cfn .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-054: The notation $\log \Lambda$ refers to the Coulomb logarithm, defined by $\Lambda = \sqrt{m_1/m_2}$ as in~\cite{Kavanagh:2020cfn .}
elements.sent-2411-055: This logarithm accounts for the range of proximities from the smaller mass where gravitational interactions with DM particles become significant.
elements.sent-2411-056: By combining Eqs.~(\ref{eq:energy_balance ) to~(\ref{eq:dE_DF ), we can obtain the evolution of the orbital frequency and phase.}}
elements.sent-2411-057: For IMRIs on circular orbits in a static DM spike, the Newtonian-order time-domain phase left until coalescence as a function of the GW frequency $f$ as derived in~\cite{Coogan:2021uqv , is given by \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \Phi^{\mathrm{S}}(f)=\Phi^{\mathrm{V}}(f) \quad \times{ } _2\mathrm{F}_1\left(1, \frac{5}{11-2 \gamma_{\mathrm{sp}}}, 1+\frac{5}{11-2 \gamma_{\mathrm{sp}}},-c_f f^{-\frac{11-2 \gamma_{\mathrm{sp}}}{3}}\right)\,. \label{Phi_S} \end{aligned}\end{equation} Here $_2F_1(a, b, c, z)$ represents the Gaussian hypergeometric function, and $\Phi^V(f)$ is the phase for the system in vacuum with the chirp mass $\mathcal{M =(m_1 m_2)^{3/5 /(m_1 + m_2)^{1/5}$ given by \begin{equation}\Phi^V (f) = \frac{1}{16} \left( \frac{1}{\pi \mathcal{M} f} \right)^{5/3}\,. \label{eq:phi_V}\end{equation} The coefficient $c_f$ is defined by \cite{Coogan:2021uqv , \begin{equation}c_f = \frac{5}{8m_1^2} \pi^{\frac{2(\gamma_{\mathrm{sp}} - 4)}{3}} (m_1 + m_2)^{\frac{1 - \gamma_{\mathrm{sp}}}{3}} r_{\mathrm{sp}}^{\gamma_{\mathrm{sp}}} \xi \rho_{sp} \log \Lambda . \label{eq:cf}\end{equation} The phase $\Phi^{\rm S (f)$ nearly follows a broken power law, characterized by a break frequency $f_{\mathrm{eq }$, where the energy loss rates from GWs and DF become equal, given by \cite{Coogan:2021uqv , \begin{equation}\label{break} f_{\mathrm{eq}} = c_f^{\frac{3}{11 - 2 \gamma_{\mathrm{sp}}}}.\end{equation}.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-058: Due to the DF effect, the inspiral process will involve fewer number cycles before coalescence, resulting in a gradual phase difference compared to a vacuum scenario.
elements.sent-2411-059: This phenomenon is referred to as dephasing, which can be mathematically expressed as: $\Delta \Phi = \Phi^{\rm V - \Phi^{\rm S}$.}
elements.sent-2411-060: The dephasing can be expanded above and below the $f_{\mathrm{eq }$ as \cite{Coogan:2021uqv , \begin{equation}\Delta \Phi(f) = \begin{cases} \Phi^{\mathrm{V}}(f), f \ll f_{\text{eq}} \frac{5 c_f \Phi^{\mathrm{V}}(f)}{2(8 - \gamma_{sp})} f^{-\frac{11 - 2 \gamma_{sp}}{3}}, f \gg f_{\text{eq}} \end{cases}. \label{eq:dephasing}\end{equation} Fig.~\ref{fig:dephasing depicts the phase difference, $\Delta \Phi = \Phi^{\rm V - \Phi^{\rm S}$, between an IMRI in a vacuum and one within a DM spike.}}}}
elements.formula-2411-013: \begin{equation}\Delta \Phi(f) =  \Phi^{V}(f), f \ll f_{eq} \frac{5 c_f \Phi^{V}(f)}{2(8 - \gamma_{sp})} f^{-\frac{11 - 2 \gamma_{sp}}{3}}, f \gg f_{eq} . \label{eq:dephasing}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-061: It is observed that $\Delta \Phi$ increases at low frequencies.
elements.sent-2411-062: This behavior arises because of the gravitational impact on the orbiting object, which is governed by the mass of DM enclosed within the orbital radius.
elements.sent-2411-063: An increased enclosed mass results in a more pronounced phase shift.
elements.sent-2411-064: Since the lower GW frequencies are associated with larger orbits, the dephasing becomes significant at these lower frequencies.

The system considered here $(m_1, m_2, \rho_{\mathrm{sp}}, \gamma_{\mathrm{sp}}) = (400 \mathrm{M}_{\odot}, 1.4 \mathrm{M}_{\odot}, 226 \mathrm{M}_{\odot}/\mathrm{pc}^3, 2.\bar{3})$ is different from \cite{Coogan:2021uqv}. The dashed line denotes the break frequency $f_{\mathrm{eq}}$ as defined in Eq.~(\ref{break}).}} \label{fig:dephasing}
elements.subsection-2411-001: Gravitational-wave strain in the frequency domain
elements.sent-2411-065: The binary system generates GWs due to variations in its quadrupole moment.
elements.sent-2411-066: Using the stationary phase approximation (SPA), the Fourier transform of the plus and cross-polarization waveforms can be expressed as~\cite{Maggiore:2007ulw \begin{equation}\tilde{h}_{+, \times} (f) = A_{+, \times}(f)\, e^{i\,\Psi^{\rm S}(f)} \label{eq:GW_strain}\end{equation} where the amplitudes of two polarizations at leading order are given by \begin{equation}A_{+}(f) = \frac{1}{D_L}\frac{(1+ \cos^2{\iota})}{2} h_0(f), A_{\times}(f) = \frac{1}{D_L}\cos{\iota} \, h_0(f), h_0(f) = \sqrt{\frac{5}{24}} \frac{M^{\frac{5}{6}}f^{-\frac{7}{6}}}{\pi^{\frac{2}{3}}} .\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-067: In these expressions, $D_L$ is the luminosity distance to the source, and $\iota$ represents the binary's inclination angle.
elements.sent-2411-068: The frequency domain SPA phase $\Psi^{\rm S (f)$ is related to the time domain phase, as a function of frequency by~\cite{Coogan:2021uqv , \begin{equation}\Psi^{\rm S}(f) = 2\pi f (t_c + t(f)) - \phi_c -\frac{\pi}{4} + \Phi^{\rm S}(f). \label{eq:Psi_S}\end{equation} where $t_c$ is the coalescence time, and $\phi_c$ is the phase at coalescence.}}
elements.sent-2411-069: The time evolution from an arbitrary initial frequency is given by \begin{equation}t(f) = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int \frac{\mathrm{d}f'}{f'} \frac{\mathrm{d} \Phi}{\mathrm{d} f}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-070: Consequently, we write the frequency-domain phase for a system in a static DM spike as \begin{equation}\Psi^{\rm S}(f) = \Psi^{V}(f) + \frac{3}{128 \eta v^5}\left[p_{k_1} v^{k_1} + p_{k_2} v^{k_2}\right], \label{eq:DM_phase}\end{equation} where $v \equiv (\pi M f)^{1/3}$ and the symmetric mass ratio $\eta = m_1 m_2/M^2$ where $M = m_1 + m_2$ is the total mass of the binary.
elements.sent-2411-071: Furthermore, $\Psi^{\rm V (f)$ represents the phase for the system in vacuum, given to leading post-Newtonian (PN) order by, \begin{equation}\Psi^{\rm V}(f) = 2\pi ft_c - \phi_c - \frac{\pi}{4} + \frac{3}{128}(\pi \mathcal{M} f)^{-5/3}. \label{eq:Psi_V}\end{equation} The second term in the right hand side of Eq.~(\ref{eq:DM_phase ) shows the deviation from the vacuum phase due to the DF effect, where the dephasing parameters $p_{k_1}$ and $p_{k_2}$ are determined by \begin{equation}p_{k_1} = \frac{-20 \,c_f\, (M \pi)^{\frac{11-2\gamma_{sp}}{3}}}{(\gamma_{sp}-8)(2\gamma_{sp} - 19)}, p_{k_2} = \frac{20 \,{c_f}^2 \,(M \pi)^{\frac{2(11-2\gamma_{sp})}{3}}}{(2\gamma_{sp}-15)(4\gamma_{sp} - 27)},\end{equation} and the powers $k_1$ and $k_2$ depend on the spike power index $\gamma_{\mathrm{sp }$ by \begin{equation}k_1 = -11 + 2\gamma_{sp}, \quad k_2 = 2(-11 + 2\gamma_{sp}).\end{equation}.}}}
elements.sent-2411-072: In order to obtain the total phase in the static DM case in the form of Eq.~(\ref{eq:DM_phase ), we expand the hypergeometric functions $_2 F_1$ present in Eq.~$(\ref{eq:Psi_S )$ up to the third order.}}
elements.sent-2411-074: Therefore, the environmental effects due to dark matter halo are more significant in the early inspiral phase when the binary system is evolving slowly.
elements.sent-2411-075: The strain of GWs from the IMRI system measured by the detector in the frequency domain is given by the linear combination of the two GW polarizations $h_{+}$ and $h_{\times}$, given by \cite{Maggiore:2007ulw , \begin{equation}\tilde{h}(f) = e^{i 2 \pi f \Delta t} \left ( \, F_{+}(\lambda, \delta, \psi) \,\tilde{h}_{+}(f) + F_{\times}(\lambda, \delta, \psi) \, \tilde{h}_{\times}(f) \right ). \label{detector_response}\end{equation} Here, $\Delta t$ is the time delay between the arrival of a gravitational wave at the Earth’s center and the detector location.}
elements.sent-2411-076: The antenna response functions $F_{+}$ and $F_{\times}$ determine the sensitivity of the detector to plus and cross polarizations, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-077: These functions are dependent on the sky position of the source, right ascension $\lambda$ and declination $\delta$, as well as polarization angle $\psi$.
elements.sent-2411-078: The Fisher matrix is a widely-used approach for predicting the statistical uncertainties in GW parameter estimation when the models for both signal and noise are known~\cite{Cramer1946, Cutler:1994ys,Vallisneri:2007ev .}
elements.sent-2411-079: In our analysis, we utilize the Fisher matrix approach to determine the $1\sigma$ uncertainties on both vacuum GR and dark matter model parameters.
elements.sent-2411-080: The GW strain data $s(t)$ recorded by a detector is typically modeled as a combination of the true gravitational wave signal $h(t, \boldsymbol{\theta )$ embedded in additive detector noise $n(t)$: \begin{equation}s(t) = h(t, \boldsymbol{\theta}) + n(t).\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-081: The likelihood function for the observed detector data, assuming stationary zero-mean Gaussian noise, is given by \begin{equation}\mathcal{L} \left(\boldsymbol{\theta}\right) = \exp \left(-\frac{1}{2} \left \langle s-h\left(\boldsymbol{\theta}\right) \mid s-h\left(\boldsymbol{\theta}\right) \right\rangle \right) .\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-082: The Fisher Information Matrix (FIM) is defined as \begin{equation}\Gamma_{pq} = -\mathrm{E}\left[\frac{\partial^2 \ln\mathcal{L}(\boldsymbol{\theta})}{\partial \theta_p \partial\theta_q}\right], \label{eq:FIM}\end{equation} where E denotes the expectation value over the noise distribution.
elements.sent-2411-083: The Fisher information quantifies how much information an observable random variable (in our case, the noisy GW signal as observed by the detectors) provides about an unknown parameter or set of parameters.
elements.sent-2411-084: It may be seen as the curvature of the log-likelihood curve, with a high value indicating sharply peaked likelihood distribution, implying less variance on the maximum likelihood estimate of the parameters; and vice versa.
elements.sent-2411-085: One appeals to the Cram\'er–Rao~\cite{Rao1945 bound according to which, the inverse of the Fisher information provides a lower bound on the variance of any unbiased estimator of the parameters.}
elements.sent-2411-086: As shown in~\cite{HELSTROM1968249 , for a signal buried in stationary Gaussian noise, the FIM above given by Eq.~\eqref{eq:FIM can be computed using the partial derivatives of the frequency-domain GW waveform with respect to the parameters: \begin{equation}\Gamma_{pq} = \left\langle \frac{\partial\tilde{h}(f)}{\partial \theta_{p}},\frac{\partial\tilde{h}(f)}{\partial \theta_{q}} \right\rangle \label{eq:Fisher_matrix} ,\end{equation} where $\tilde{h (f, \boldsymbol{\theta )$ is the frequency-domain GW waveform template defined by the set of parameters $\boldsymbol{\theta}$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-087: The inner product between two functions, $a(f)$ and $b(f)$, weighted by the detector noise, is defined as: \begin{equation}\langle a,b\rangle=4 \mathcal{R}\int_{f_{\rm low}}^{\rm f_{\rm high}}\frac{a(f)\,b^*(f)+a^*(f)\, b(f)}{S_n(f)}\,df \,,\end{equation} where and ``$*$'' represents the complex conjugation, and $S_n(f )$ is the one-sided power spectral density (PSD) of the noise.
elements.formula-2411-027: \begin{equation}\langle a,b\rangle=4 R\int_{f_{\rm low}}^{\rm f_{\rm high}}\frac{a(f)\,b^*(f)+a^*(f)\, b(f)}{S_n(f)}\,df \,,\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-088: The signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) for a given $\tilde{h}$ and a specific detector PSD $S_n(f)$ is given by \begin{equation}\rho = \langle \tilde{h}, \tilde{h} \rangle^{1/2},\end{equation} which indicates the relative strength of the detector response in relation to the detector noise.
elements.sent-2411-089: In the limit of high SNR, the inverse of the FIM provides the error covariance matrix $\Sigma_{pq =(\Gamma_{pq )^{-1}$ and the square root of the diagonal elements of $\Sigma_{pq}$ gives the 1$\sigma$ error for each parameter: $\sigma_p = \sqrt{\Sigma_{pp }$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-090: In the case of multiband observations, where signals are detected jointly by both a space-based detector and a ground-based detector, the total FIM is obtained by summing the individual FIM from each detector \cite{Datta:2020vcj : \begin{equation}\Gamma^{\mathrm{Combined}}_{pq} = \sum_{j} \Gamma_{pq}^{j},\end{equation} where $\Gamma_{pq ^j$ indicates the Fisher matrix associated with the j-th detector.}}
elements.sent-2411-091: The SNR for the network of detectors is defined as \begin{equation}\rho^{\rm Combined}=\sqrt{\sum_j {\rho^j}^2},\end{equation} where $\rho^j$ denotes the SNR of j-th detector.

elements.sent-2411-167: In this paper, we investigated the capability of multiband gravitational wave observations to constrain the properties of static dark matter spikes around IMRIs using a Fisher analysis framework.
elements.sent-2411-168: Specifically, we studied IMRI systems embedded in static dark matter spike profiles and examined how multiband observations, combining space-based and 3G ground-based detectors, can improve the estimation of both vacuum and dark matter parameters.
elements.sent-2411-169: The study focused on systems characterized by varying masses of the primary black hole and fixed secondary mass of $1.4\mathrm{M _{\odot}$.}
elements.sent-2411-170: We incorporated the effects of dynamical friction which gives rise to the correction to the inspiral part of the Newtonian GW waveform phase in relation to the vacuum phase.
elements.sent-2411-171: This effect appears at the leading order into the GW phase compared to the leading contribution coming from GR, rendering them significant during the early inspiral phase of the binary system.
elements.sent-2411-172: For these effects to be detectable, they must accumulate over many orbital cycles, which primarily occur during the extended inspiral phase.
elements.sent-2411-173: Therefore, it is crucial to consider observations from future space-based detectors, as they will capture the accumulated effects of dark matter over a significant number of cycles from the IMRIs.
elements.sent-2411-174: By utilizing the complementary sensitivity of space-based detectors such as GWSat and 3G ground-based detectors like CE and ET, we achieved significantly better error estimates on the static dark matter spike parameters, including the spike density $\rho_{\mathrm{sp }$ and the power-law index $\gamma_{\rm sp}$, compared to individual detectors alone.}
elements.sent-2411-175: We showed that while CE and ET when used independently, provide limited constraints on the parameters $\gamma_{\rm sp}$ and $\rho_{\rm{sp }$, incorporating GWSat in a multiband approach yields substantial improvements in parameter estimation.}
elements.sent-2411-176: In particular, our results demonstrated that the joint multiband approach enhances the constraints on $\gamma_{\rm sp}$ and $\rho_{\mathrm{sp }$ by factors of approximately $10^{6}$ and $10^{3}$, respectively, relative to observations with only ground-based detectors.}
elements.sent-2411-177: Moreover, the addition of priors on the parameters obtained from the GWSat observations while estimating errors with CE and ET significantly improves the bounds on the parameters.\par \textcolor{black {However, the IMRI systems are highly asymmetric, and the contribution of higher modes becomes important for more accurate estimations of the parameter uncertainty.}}
elements.sent-2411-178: We would like to explore the effects of the contribution of the higher modes to our analysis in a future study.
elements.sent-2411-179: Also, it will be interesting to extend our analysis for eccentric orbits and for spinning binaries.
elements.sent-2411-180: Furthermore, we like to extend our studies to effective field theory models of dark matter (e.g., the one considered \cite{Bhattacharyya:2023kbh ).}
elements.sent-2411-181: For this, we need to systematically compute the GW phase and carry out the Fisher analysis.
elements.sent-2411-182: Last but not least, we like to perform a thorough Bayesian analysis to get a more realistic bound on the dark matter parameter using multi-band observations.
elements.sent-2411-183: We hope to report on it soon.

elements.sent-2411-187: The presence of dark matter influences the orbital evolution of the secondary compact object through dynamic friction, which leads to a phase shift in the gravitational waveform compared to the vacuum case.
elements.sent-2411-188: Our analysis shows that the proposed Indian space-based detector GWSat, operating in the deciHz frequency band, provides the most stringent constraints on the dark matter spike parameters, as IMRIs spend a significant portion of their inspiral phase within its sensitivity range.
elements.sent-2411-189: While third-generation ground-based detectors such as the Einstein Telescope and Cosmic Explorer offer additional constraints, their contribution is somewhat limited, particularly for higher-mass systems where the signal duration in their frequency bands is shorter.
elements.sent-2411-190: However, for systems with detector-frame total masses $M_z < 400 \rm M_{\odot}$, Cosmic Explorer and Einstein Telescope could improve the estimation of the chirp mass, symmetric mass ratio, luminosity distance, and dark matter spike power-law index by more than $15\%$.
elements.sent-2411-191: Nonetheless, their impact on the constraint of spike density is minimal.
elements.sent-2411-192: These results highlight the crucial role of deciHz space-based detectors in probing dark matter interactions with gravitational wave sources.
elements.sent-2411-193: Such environments could measurably impact the emitted GWs, altering the observed waveforms and providing indirect evidence of the presence and distribution of dark matter~\cite{Macedo:2013qea, Eda:2013gg, Eda:2014kra, Yue:2017iwc,Yue:2018vtk,Hannuksela:2019vip,Cardoso:2019rou,Kavanagh:2020cfn,Coogan:2021uqv,PhysRevD.104.104042,PhysRevD.104.124082,Becker:2022wlo,Baryakhtar:2022hbu,Cardoso:2022whc,Singh:2022wvw,Destounis:2022obl,Kadota:2023wlm,Nichols:2023ufs, AbhishekChowdhuri:2023rfv, Bhattacharyya:2023kbh, Rahman:2023sof,Duque:2023seg,CanevaSantoro:2023aol,Speeney:2024mas,Aurrekoetxea:2024cqd,Zhang:2024ugv,Bertone:2024wbn,Spieksma:2024voy,Duque:2024mfw,Cheng:2024mgl .}
elements.sent-2411-194: Feedback mechanisms during binary inspiral dynamically reshape the DM profile.
elements.sent-2411-195: As a stellar-mass companion spirals into an IMBH, energy transfer via dynamical friction depletes the DM spike over time.
elements.sent-2411-196: Consequently, the DM density near the secondary object is reduced, which weakens dynamical friction and modifies the inspiral rate compared to the static case.
elements.sent-2411-197: When feedback on the DM distribution is taken into account, the dephasing caused by dynamical friction is significantly lower than in the static scenario, which is often discussed in the literature \cite{Karydas:2024fcn, Mukherjee:2023lzn, Cole:2022yzw, Kavanagh:2024lgq .}
elements.sent-2411-198: Nonetheless, the DM dephasing effect might still be substantial enough to be observed by future gravitational wave detectors~\cite{Cole:2022ucw, Coogan:2021uqv .}
elements.sent-2411-199: To assess the detectability of IMRIs in such evolving DM distributions, Ref.~\cite{Coogan:2021uqv conducted a simulation-based analysis to evaluate their measurement prospects with LISA and their distinguishability from vacuum IMRIs.}
elements.sent-2411-200: This study also proposed an analytical phenomenological model to describe the GW phase evolution in the frequency domain, facilitating Bayesian parameter estimation.
elements.sent-2411-201: A recent study~\cite{CanevaSantoro:2023aol conducted a Bayesian analysis of the first gravitational-wave catalog (GWTC-1) by LIGO-Virgo and found no significant evidence of environmental effects on the observed signals, but the results highlight the limitations of current detectors in detecting these subtle influences, particularly during the early inspiral phase where environmental effects are most likely to be identifiable.}
elements.sent-2411-202: We use the phenomenological model for GW phase for an IMRI inside a dynamic dark matter spike environment as described~\cite{Coogan:2021uqv .}
elements.sent-2411-203: We study how well the binary parameters, as well as dark matter parameters, could be constrained by combining the observations from an Indian deciHz space-based detector GWSat, and 3G ground-based detectors CE and ET using Fisher analysis framework.

elements.sent-2411-205: The synergy between GWSat and 3G ground-based detectors strengthens error estimates, particularly for systems with $M_z < 400 \rm M_{\odot}$.

elements.sent-2411-209: We consider an IMRI system comprising a stellar-mass compact object spiraling into an IMBH that is enveloped by a DM spike.
elements.sent-2411-210: The mass of the IMBH is denoted by $m_1$, while the mass of the small compact object is indicated by $m_2$.
elements.sent-2411-211: The total mass of the binary system is given by $M = m_1 + m_2$, and he orbital radius of the small compact object is symbolized by $r_2$.
elements.sent-2411-212: Initially, if the DM halo follows a cuspy profile characterized by $\rho(r)\propto r^{-\alpha_{\rm ini }$ with $0 \leq \alpha_{\rm ini \leq 2$, this distribution of dark matter will be altered due to adiabatic growth of the central IMBH, leading to the formation of a dense spike~\cite{Gondolo:1999ef .}}}
elements.sent-2411-213: Following~\cite{Eda:2014kra , we consider that the DM spike distribution is spherically symmetric DM with a simple power law: \begin{equation}\rho_{\text{DM}}(r) = \begin{cases} \rho_{\text{sp}} \left( \frac{r_{\text{sp}}}{r} \right)^{\gamma_{\text{sp}}} \text{for } r_{\text{in}} \leq r \leq r_{\text{sp}} 0 \text{for } r < r_{\text{in}} \end{cases}, \label{eq:static_density_distribution}\end{equation} where $r$ denotes the distance from the center of the IMBH.}
elements.formula-2411-037: \begin{equation}\rho_{DM}(r) =  \rho_{sp} \left( \frac{r_{sp}}{r} \right)^{\gamma_{sp}} for  r_{in} \leq r \leq r_{sp} 0 for  r < r_{in} , \label{eq:static_density_distribution}\end{equation}

elements.sent-2411-300: Our findings show that GWSat alone provides the strongest constraints on dark matter parameters, particularly the spike density normalization $\rho_{\rm{sp }$ and the power-law index $\gamma_{\rm{sp }$, outperforming ground-based detectors due to its longer observation window at low frequencies.}}
elements.sent-2411-301: The addition of CE and ET improves parameter bounds for chirp mass $(\mathcal{M _z)$, symmetric mass ratio $(\nu)$, luminosity distance $(D_L)$, and dark matter spike index $(\gamma_{\rm{sp )$ by more than 15$\%$ for systems with $M_z < 400 \rm M_{\odot}$ at sky locations where CE records the highest SNR.}}}
elements.sent-2411-302: However, this improvement is limited to approximately 5$\%$ at low-SNR locations, highlighting the dependence of parameter estimation on detector sensitivity across different regions of the sky.
elements.sent-2411-303: Moreover, the inclusion of CE and ET has a negligible impact on the constraints for $\rho_{\mathrm{sp }$, with an improvement of less than 0.1$\%$, regardless of sky location.}
elements.sent-2411-304: This result reinforces the necessity of space-based observations, as GWSat remains the dominant contributor to probing dark matter properties through gravitational waves.
elements.sent-2411-305: However, there are certain caveats in this study.
elements.sent-2411-306: For simplicity, we assume that GWSat observes the IMRI system during the final year of its inspiral before the merger.
elements.sent-2411-307: This ensures a smooth transition of the system into the sensitive frequency band of ET, followed by CE, after it exits the GWSat band, thereby enabling multiband observations.
elements.sent-2411-308: This work primarily serves as a proof of principle, highlighting the potential of a deci-Hz detector in constraining dark matter properties using IMRI signals.
elements.sent-2411-309: We also assume that the signals detected by GWSat and ground-based observatories (CE and ET) can be confidently associated with the same IMRI system, allowing Fisher matrices to be combined for a multiband observation.
elements.sent-2411-310: In practice, identifying and matching signals across different detectors is a complex task, particularly for long-duration space-based observations where the data stream includes instrumental noise and foreground of overlapping signals.
elements.sent-2411-311: This issue has been discussed in previous studies (e.g., Ref~\cite{Lackeos:2023eub in the context of LISA).}
elements.sent-2411-312: While we do not address these practical challenges, recent studies such as sequential simulation-based inference methods like Peregrine \cite{Bhardwaj:2023xph , have been proposed to resolve overlapping signals, while normalizing flow-based techniques~\cite{Langendorff:2022fzq offer promising approaches for parameter estimation in complex detection scenarios.}}
elements.sent-2411-313: Adapting these methods to the deci-Hz band could significantly improve signal identification and enhance parameter constraints in multiband gravitational wave observations.
elements.sent-2411-314: Also, it will be interesting to extend our analysis for eccentric orbits and spinning binaries.
elements.sent-2411-315: We hope to report on some of these soon.
